patterns.name: Reported Speech Time Words
patterns.what: Tends to forget to change time and pointer words when converting direct speech into reported speech.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: SHRIV8209J kept 'yesterday' instead of changing it to 'the previous day'.
SHRIV8209J left 'next week' as is, rather than shifting it to 'the following week'.
SHRIV8209J used 'this' instead of changing it to 'that' for the reported speech.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see reporting words, treat them as a signal to check your time and pointer words:
- **'yesterday'** changes to **'the previous day'**
- **'next week'** changes to **'the following week'**
- **'this'** changes to **'that'**

patterns.name: Dropping Small Words
patterns.what: Occasionally leaves out descriptive words or swaps specific pronouns when rewriting a sentence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: SHRIV8209J missed out the word 'firmly', which slightly changed the meaning of the parents' refusal.
SHRIV8209J wrote 'the teacher' instead of keeping the more specific 'their teacher'.
SHRIV8209J dropped the word 'sudden' when describing Sam's illness.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you finish writing your new sentence, do a quick matching check with the original.
- Look closely for adjectives (like **'sudden'**) and adverbs (like **'firmly'**).
- Double-check that ownership words like **'their'** haven't accidentally turned into **'the'**.

patterns.name: Forgotten Commas
patterns.what: Sometimes misses the comma needed to separate a starting phrase from the main part of the sentence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: SHRIV8209J forgot to place a comma after 'heavy rain' to separate the reason from the result.
SHRIV8209J missed the comma after 'throwing the ball' before introducing the main action.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When a sentence starts with a phrase that sets the scene or gives a reason, you usually need a comma before the main action begins.
- Read your sentence aloud and listen for the natural pause.
- When you see starters like **'Due to'** or **'No matter'**, remember to drop a comma right after the introductory part!
